institutions.institution_blurb: First rector of the Collegiate School (Yale), 1701-1707.

cross_connections.display_label: trained Pierson, Yale's first rector, at Milford
Both founding trustees of Yale 1701; Pierpont secured the charter.
First Yale tutor; Thomas Hooker's grandson.

narrative_blocks.header: Taught Yale's first class in his own home
narrative_blocks.body: Elected first rector on November 11, 1701, he could not leave his Killingworth congregation, so the first Yale students were taught in his home parsonage. The first student, Jacob Hemingway of East Haven, "solus was all the College the first half-year." By 1704 there were twenty students, most boarding at the Pierson home. He died in office on March 5, 1707.

narrative_blocks.header: First Yale tutor: Daniel Hooker, Thomas Hooker's grandson
narrative_blocks.body: The first Yale tutor he hired was Daniel Hooker — a grandson of Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford. Hooker also became Yale's first B.A. graduate in 1703. The second tutor, John Hart, received the first Yale B.A. in course in 1703 and was ordained at East Guilford in 1706 in a ceremony that included Rev. James Pierpont Sr..

narrative_blocks.header: Studied theology under Rev. Roger Newton at Milford
narrative_blocks.body: After graduating Harvard 1668 he went to Milford CT to study theology under Rev. Roger Newton — the founding pastor of Farmington, trained by Thomas Hooker himself. At Milford he also met Thomas Buckingham, who would later join him as one of the original Yale trustees.

cross_connections.display_label: Both New Haven founders; both rejected the Half-Way Covenant.

narrative_blocks.header: Every principle clash ended in a new town
narrative_blocks.body: He came to New England in 1639, helped found Southampton on Long Island (1640), then led a group to found Branford CT (1644), and finally Newark NJ (1666). Each move was driven by his rejection of compromise on church membership standards. At Newark he pursued the theocratic vision of New Haven Colony that John Davenport had established — both men opponents of the Half-Way Covenant.

narrative_blocks.header: Son founded Yale; daughter married its co-founder's son
narrative_blocks.body: His son Abraham Pierson Jr. became the first rector of Yale College (1701-1707), teaching the first Yale students in his own home at Killingworth CT. His daughter Abigail Pierson married John Davenport Jr. — son of Rev. John Davenport Sr., who had envisioned a college in New Haven decades before Yale was chartered. Both lines converge on the Yale founding.

notable.notable_blurb: Congregational minister; co-founded Southampton, Branford, and Newark

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
